name: Kinich Ahau
description: Kinich Ahau was the Mayan sun god, a powerful and vital deity representing the life-giving force of the sun. He was closely associated with rulership, kingship, and divine power. Often depicted with large, square eyes and a filed incisor tooth marked with a sign representing the sun, Kinich Ahau embodied the masculine principle and the day. He was believed to journey across the sky each day in a solar barge, battling the forces of the underworld each night to be reborn at dawn. As the sun god, he was responsible for providing warmth and light, essential for agriculture and the sustenance of life. He was also linked to the elite and royal lineage, with Mayan kings often identifying themselves with Kinich Ahau to legitimize their rule. His image appears frequently in Mayan art and architecture, particularly in depictions of royal ceremonies and calendrical events. Offerings and rituals were performed to honor him, ensuring the continuation of the sun's cycle and the prosperity of the Mayan people. He represents the embodiment of light, heat, and the cyclical nature of time and existence. Kinich Ahau is sometimes considered an aspect of Itzamná, the creator god, further emphasizing his significance in the Mayan pantheon.
subtitle: The Mayan Sun God, Royal Patron

keyMyths.title: The Sun's Daily Journey
keyMyths.description: Kinich Ahau travels across the sky each day in his solar barge, bringing light and warmth to the world. At night, he descends into the underworld, battling demons to ensure his rebirth.
keyMyths.source: Various Mayan codices and stelae interpretations

keyMyths.title: Kinich Ahau and Royal Power
keyMyths.description: Mayan kings associated themselves with Kinich Ahau to solidify their divine right to rule. They often wore symbols of the sun god and performed rituals in his honor.

keyMyths.title: Offerings to Kinich Ahau
keyMyths.description: The Mayan people offered sacrifices and performed rituals to appease Kinich Ahau and ensure the continuation of the sun's cycle. These offerings included food, incense, and sometimes, human sacrifices.

sources.author: Michael D. Coe
